quote: But go ahead, sir. I didn't mean to cut you off.
stewart_read: Kelly is female — she introduced herself by name at 00:10 ('Is this Kelly? Yeah. This is Kelly.'). Addressing her as 'sir' is a gender error. On a warm, self-motivated customer this early in the call, it's a micro-fracture rather than a deal-breaker, but it signals Pike was not fully tracking the person on the other end of the line.
alternative_hypothesis: Pike may have been reading from a lead record with ambiguous gender data and defaulted to 'sir' as a filler — reactive rather than a deliberate choice.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play the clip and ask Pike to identify the error. The coaching point is not the word 'sir' — it's the habit of not anchoring on the customer's name and voice before the call starts. Per energy_match, the rep should be matching the customer's register, which requires actually tracking who they're talking to.

quote: why would I not do that. Of course. If that's true.
stewart_read: This is the call's highest-leverage moment and Pike walked past it. Kelly has already done the cost-benefit math and landed on 'yes' — but she's holding a conditional ('if that's true') that is an open credibility check. The strategic move here is to convert the conditional into a commitment: anchor on the Tesla-certified installer framing from the intro, point to the neighbor solar installations Pike had already noted on the roof view, and let Kelly's own logic close the loop. Instead, Pike moves directly into verify. The enthusiasm signal was real; the amplification was absent.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Pike may have deliberately not over-sold here, knowing the closer will handle the full value case — keeping setter scope. This is plausible but weak, because the 'if that's true' hedge is a legitimacy question, not a product question. Legitimacy is setter-scope content.
coaching_implication: Show Pike the clip and ask: 'What did Kelly just tell you?' The answer is she sold herself — and then asked you to confirm she wasn't being fooled. The coaching move is a one-sentence legitimacy anchor (Tesla-certified framing, neighbor installations already visible) before moving to verify. No numbers needed, no product pitch — just close the conditional.

quote: I mean, it depends. Like, in the summer, I'll pay, you know, like, $70. In the winter, it can be closer to $15,200.
stewart_read: Kelly gives a bill range with a meaningful winter spike. Pike's response is 'Okay. So it can kinda double in the summer there?' — which misreads the data (Kelly said winter is higher, not summer) and then drops the anchor entirely with 'K. Great job.' The bill was captured as data and immediately abandoned. Per the schema, capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. The flip opportunity here was direct: Kelly's winter bill is the pain point, and Pike had the number in hand.
coaching_implication: This is the floor's universal coaching gap per the schema — every rep captures and drops. Use this clip as the concrete example for Pike. The coaching question: 'After Kelly told you her winter bill, what did you do with it?' Walk Pike through the flip structure: acknowledge the number, name the pain it represents, redirect it toward the value case. No fabricated savings figures — the pain the number represents is the leverage.

quote: No. [to credit question] Perfect. And you qualify just fine.
stewart_read: Pike asks the credit qualifier at the correct threshold (670, matching the schema) and self-qualifies on Kelly's stated 'no' without any verification. Per qualifiers.credit_qualifier.spencer_operator_note, stated credit ≠ pulled credit, and the policy question of whether setters should DQ on stated score is explicitly unresolved in the schema. Pike's 'you qualify just fine' is a confident declaration based on a self-report. This is not a coaching failure if Ion's policy is setter-DQs-on-stated — but if the policy is closer-pulls-the-credit, Pike has just made a promise the closer may not be able to keep.
alternative_hypothesis: If Ion's policy is that the setter's job is to ask and pass through regardless of stated score, then Pike's handling is correct procedure and the only coaching note is the over-confident 'you qualify just fine' phrasing.
coaching_implication: Flag for the manager, not for Pike directly — this is a policy question that needs Kenny's resolution (see qualifiers.credit_qualifier.policy_question_for_kenny). Once the policy is confirmed, the coaching direction is clear: either Pike is correct and the phrasing is fine, or Pike is making promises the closer can't honor. Don't coach Pike on this until the policy is settled.

quote: And do you guys come out and inspect, like, the roof and everything and make sure that's in a good condition before you do any install?
stewart_read: Kelly asks a process question about roof inspection — this is not a replacement signal, it's a customer checking whether Ion does due diligence before installing. Pike answers correctly ('100%. Yeah. That's, we do a site survey'). The coaching gap here is what the question reveals: Pike confirmed roof type (asphalt shingle) via satellite view at 01:44 but never captured roof age. Roof age is a required verify item with a hard DQ threshold (over 15 years, no replacement planned). Kelly's inspection question suggests she may have some awareness of roof condition — the natural follow-up was 'How old is your roof?' and Pike did not ask it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Pike may have assessed roof age visually via satellite and judged it acceptable, choosing not to ask. This is possible but unverifiable from the transcript — and the schema requires the item to be captured, not estimated.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Pike: 'Do you know how old Kelly's roof is?' The answer is no. Walk through why roof age matters (hard DQ threshold, roofing cross-sell opportunity if replacement is needed). The coaching move is simple: after confirming roof type, ask 'And do you know roughly how old it is?' — one question, 10 seconds, closes a verify gap and opens a potential cross-sell signal.

quote: once we get the usage, my next step is just to get that design built out, and then we'll have one of my local specialists just meet with you to go over that design
stewart_read: The phrase 'meet with you' and the later 'drop by' at 06:44 introduce ambiguity about whether the design-review appointment is in-person or by phone. The schema specifies the appointment is a phone call (button_up.phone_appointment_specified). The site survey is correctly in-person — Pike's reference to 'we'll schedule this survey, and they'll come out' at 05:10 is accurate. But 'drop by' at 06:44 conflates the two, and Kelly may be expecting an in-person design review. If she's expecting a house visit and gets a phone call, the no-show risk rises.
alternative_hypothesis: Pike may be using 'drop by' loosely to mean 'show up on your calendar' rather than literally appear at the door. Kelly did not push back, which could indicate she understood the phone context. This is plausible but the ambiguity is real and the schema is explicit.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Pike to describe what the appointment actually is. Then play the 'drop by' clip and ask whether Kelly knows it's a phone call. The fix is one sentence in the button-up: 'This will be a phone call — no need to be home, just somewhere you can sit down and look at the design on your computer.' That sentence also sets up the no-driving anchor the button-up currently lacks.

stewart_read: Deepgram has transcribed Kelly's kWh readings as date strings — the speech-to-text engine misheard numeric sequences and rendered them as dates. Pike accepts these without apparent verification. At 06:28, Pike asks Kelly to repeat 'March' and Kelly reads back 'January' — Pike accepts 'January' and moves on. The design team will receive a usage history that includes at minimum five garbled data points. If the system is sized on corrupted kWh data, the design will be inaccurate and the closer's proposal will be built on a bad foundation.
alternative_hypothesis: Pike may have been writing down the actual numbers Kelly spoke aloud and the transcript error is Deepgram's, not Pike's. This is possible — the audio may have been clear. However, the March/January confusion at 06:28-06:36 is a live transcript artifact that suggests at least one data point was misheard in real time.
coaching_implication: Flag for the handoff brief immediately — the design team should be alerted that the kWh data from this call may contain transcription errors and should verify against Kelly's actual bill before sizing the system. For Pike's coaching: after a verbal readback session, confirm the total or the highest month back to the customer ('So your highest month was around 598 kWh in August — does that sound right?') as a data-integrity check. One confirmation question catches garbled data before it reaches the design team.

quote: So just look out for that text.
stewart_read: The button-up is missing three required elements: (1) no 'is that fair' verbal handshake, (2) no explicit phone/no-driving/computer anchor, (3) no personalized text tieback — the text promise is generic ('look out for that text') with no reference to anything Kelly said on the call. The schema treats the 'is that fair' handshake as predictive of show rate. The text tieback is required to reference something specific the customer said. Kelly gave Pike multiple tieback options: the friend referral, the winter bill spike, the neighbor solar installations. None appear in the close.
coaching_implication: This is the cleanest coaching clip on the call — play it and ask Pike to walk through the button-up checklist. The gap is not one missed line; it's three. The priority fix is the 'is that fair' handshake (one sentence, highest show-rate impact) and the phone/computer anchor (one sentence, sets correct expectations). The text tieback is the easiest: 'I'll send you a text with the details — I'll tie it back to that winter bill you mentioned.' Thirty seconds of additional close time, materially better appointment quality.
